sentences.text: The event of the Austrian Anschluss was, for me, very easily understandable, as I understood German fluently and perfectly.
So starting from the speeches of Hitler and even the newspaper of the which was the Nazi official journal, and Zeitung.
And I could read because, first of all, we learned in school the Gothic print and writing.
So probably due to that, my knowledge of the press comments and general announcements, and so on, in German was no difficult, not-- I didn't have to translate it.
I could understand it.
The-- shortly after the Anschluss-- obviously, my father and the family was very concerned for my uncle, the doctor, who wasn't married, even, to get him out of Austria.

And it's interesting how, with a Hungarian passport, a Jew, my father-- you will probably take a photo of the very few pictures I've got left-- did not particularly have the appearance of a Jew-- could travel up into Austria from Hungary with a Hungarian passport and be let in without any problem.
Probably not even fearing his own safety, went and collected my uncle.
Probably he took him some papers along already and brought him back to Sapron, where he stayed a while with our family.
I mean, he used to come and see us on weekends.
He had a, a car and used to come down from Austria just to have a Sunday meal or for a Shabbat.
So that way, he brought him out.
And no doubt, my father hasn't told me all the details.
I was too young to understand it, what he saw and what was going on.
And therefore, that event is interesting how a Jew from Hungary, without fear, could go up, like my father did, to bring his brother up, which was no problem.

As it happens, this uncle of mine obviously couldn't practice as a doctor officially in Hungary and went up to Budapest where he started to manufacture ladies' cosmetic products, which he was very successful with.

In fact, at the end of it, he was not deported to Auschwitz or any camps, but got caught up in the Munka Tabor and eventually was taken to Felixdorf, which is a, a small place in Austria, also was kind of a camp, of concentration camp, of a minor size.
And he survived there.
He went back to Budapest after the war and had a wife and two children.
The wife and he died, unfortunately-- died rather early, 1960.
I took him out to Australia, where I lived after the war between 1951 and '58.
I brought him up after the '56 revolution.
And unfortunately, within four years, he died.
He suddenly had a heart attack.
His wife survived in Warsaw, subsequently died.
She had leukemia or something.
And the two children, two cousins, do exist today in Melbourne, Australia.
The other-- to do with the Austrian Anschluss, which is clearly in my mind, there were Jews left in Vienna right up till late 1942.

Most of the Austrian Jews were taken to Poland because of-- the time it started in Austria was before the killing machinery was put into effect and were sent to what they called Litzmannstadt, which is Lodz, the Lodz Ghetto.

And there were some who apparently were hidden and then-- but they seeked out even the most assimilated Jews who became converted, and so on, because Hitler did not consider anybody-- he considered everybody Jewish who had even a single grandparent as a Jew.
I mean, this is a historical fact.
Anyway, so these people in Vienna, who-- diminished by the fact that a lot of them still had the opportunity to run away.
And I must say that the Austrian and German Jews were the only two nations' Jews who had still the opportunity to go.
Because it was before the Second World War broke out.
And at that time, the Germans were interested just to get rid of the Jews-- take their assets, get rid of them.
And they were not ready yet to kill them.
That only event happened after the Evian conference, when Hitler saw that the Western powers were not in the slightest interested in the fate of Jews.
And he knew he had a free hand.
His ambassador came back and said, you can do what you like.
That was a serious accusation for the Western nations.
I must say, as an interesting interruption here, I've seen a film, a documentary film on the BBC.
I can't remember its title.
It had to do with the Hungarian Jews' negotiations of 10,000 trucks for a million Jews.
The story is well-documented in books and otherwise, so I'm not going to go into it.

But there was a very interesting comment at the end of that film, which said that even if the deal would have gone through, which was most unlikely because the allies wouldn't have agreed and so on, who would've taken a million Jews?
Nobody wanted to.
And this is absolutely true.
And I must say here a word of-- maybe coming from the wrong source, being me-- a commendation about Franco.
Franco has never handed out his Jews.

Franco, in a silent manner, shut his eye to a lot of Jews who ran away from Belgium, Germany, Austria via France to Spain, and continued from there on either to Portugal or, or South America or wherever, and actively-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] maybe not supported it, because obviously he was an ally of Germany.
And Hitler supported him in his civil war.
Nevertheless, being a fascist-- and whatever people say, has-- had the decency of tolerance to let Jews survive and escape via Spain.
This is an, an absolute fact.

In, in, in fact, I, I know people who, in Gibraltar-- who told me that the, the governor of Andalusia, which is a southern province of Spain, who was a general in Franco's army has actively, on Franco's say-so, assisted the Moroccan Jews' escape in 1950 via Spain.
And they let them stay in temporary camps there until they went on to either Israel or France.
So one has got to give credit where it's due.

Now, for the Austrian Jews, I literally only know as much that the people who rented our big garage premises-- which later become-- became under Communist times, the central headquarters of the Communist party's garages-- used to go up weekly to, to, to Vienna.
So we, we sent to some people who we knew.
We didn't know they could not have easy access to Jews.
Because the Austrians were probably the worst anti-Semites.
And I think I ought to record it for posterity that it is a statistical fact that the Austrian [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] people represented 8% of the German army, but over 50% of the war criminals.
Let it remain at that.
Now, the-- so therefore, what I remember is that, whilst you could get all sorts of fruit and foodstuffs in Hungary, there was a shortage.
You couldn't get bananas, coconuts, [LAUGHS] and [LAUGHS] maybe one other product.
You know, the stupid things which one remembers.
And I remember that my father always gave instructions to his tenant, whenever you go up to Vienna, you bring back.
This is already after the Anschluss.

Nevertheless, what we-- there was apparently somehow organized that the Jews who were ostracized in, in, in, in Austria-- the people didn't dare to say good morning to them or, or, or knock on their doors-- and stayed till the-- 1941, '42, or '40, '41, '42, were allowed to receive what they called [GERMAN].
These were like a registered envelope in which you were allowed to put in a hundred grams-- in other words, 10 decagrams.

Or if you like to convert it into pounds, it's less than a quarter of a pound of foodstuff, mainly meat, either raw or cooked, which was within two hours in Vienna and probably delivered the same day or the next day to various addresses.
And I remember every week, we kept on addressing dozens and dozens of, of, of envelopes, of these little package envelopes, and sending food up to the people who we didn't even know.
We just got lists.
Probably they individually or the community supplied it.
That is what I remember very clearly.
We know that eventually all of them got deported.
And very little was known in Hungary.
Even though we were 60 kilometers from Vienna, we did not know.
We were kept in the dark.

And there is a serious allegation I must record as far as the Jewish Judenrat committee of Budapest are at fault, or in serious-- under serious accusation that they, for their personal safety-- which I can understand, that everybody tries to look after themselves.
But in that case, they shouldn't take on communal duties.

Then they should resign-- have persistently and actively denied any knowledge of Auschwitz or of any concentration camp for that reason, and kept on reassuring the provincial jury, and for that reason, probably the Budapest jury.
In other words, actively carrying out the best wishes of Eichmann and the SS that-- not to cause panic, and do everything in an orderly manner.
And if you really think what a fantastic organization it was, that you step into a country, OK, it was an allied country and the Hungarians were more than happy to cooperate.
The Germans didn't need SS troops to carry on the deportations.

They were-- [LAUGHS] just because recently we read and hear a lot about Bosnia and Yugoslavia, I want to record it that the Bosnian Muslims had an SS battalion, which is known, who were the people who were guarding the trains to Auschwitz and travelled along it.
The world doesn't know.
And I know about it.
And, in fact, I think I've seen an article once in the where that was recorded.
People think that people are being badly treated for no reason at all.
What the Serbs have suffered is unbelievable.
Tito was a Croat, as it happened, but he was a Communist.
And this is maybe politicizing, but it has to do with Jews.
There were two nations who really, actively assisted the Jews as such.
And these were the Danes, where the king himself, as it's known, said, I put on a red-- a yellow star if my Jewish people have to, and helped them to escape to Sweden, and the Serbs.
Serbs being great Catholics-- and I don't hold up any great torches, that the Greek Orthodox, who were basically the Russian religion, were so tremendously nice over all the centuries to Jews.
But as it happens, maybe because the Serbs were suffering under the, the, the, the persecution of the Ustase of the Croats-- who are the worst Nazis.
I mean, there they hardly had to deport people because they killed them on the spot like in Lithuania and Latvia.
These were the couple of places where they didn't have to bother to deport them.
They killed them on the spot.
And I don't think it is not worth remembering that today's president of Croatia has written a book in which he is basically dismissing the whole Holocaust.
And maybe today for political reasons he is backtracking on it.
But the Croatians have got a terrible history.
And I'm very sorry if individual people got killed or maimed there.
But they have got a great responsibility what they did during the Second World War, what happened now.
And this is far from understood by the Western nations, who don't know the ethnic differences and the background from where all this trouble has come from.
The Balkans have always been the cause of world wars.
The first world war started there.
So that I leave out because this is not really a Jewish matter.
Right, now, coming back after Austria, the next event which we were well aware of was when-- after Chamberlain's appeasement in Munich, the Germans went into Czechoslovakia.
The reason for it is that my mother's-- one, two, three brothers out of four, all married from Slovakia.
Czechoslovakia was one of the most prosperous countries before the war and had one of the best products.
Its Jewish community is longstanding, going back Prague and so on.
And I remember vividly that my oldest uncle-- he wasn't the oldest of the brothers and sisters, but the oldest brother.
His name was Alexander Holzer.
His in-laws suddenly appeared in [NON-ENGLISH] as soon as the Germans came in in '39.
A very nice elderly couple who were large agricultural landowners came and lived with my uncle and aunt and their daughter there all the years-- very nice, gentle people.
And that gave me the impression what was going on in Slovakia.
But obviously, part of Slovakia then, under the 1940 Vienna treaty, was passed on to Hungary.

And basically, the Czechoslovak jury, those who were not annexed to, to, to Hungary, had an easy way to-- because they spoke mostly Hungarian, as well-- to come over to Hungary between the years of '39 and '44.
But they've already-- and they lived as non-Jews and with-- or with Jewish families, depending on what papers they had.
And really, they were already better equipped when it came to the 1944 troubles in Hungary.
They ran back to Slovakia.
And they went into bunkers in hiding because they were already under this constant threat of being caught-- already for, for, for, for, four or five years, from '39 to '44.
Anyway, it's interesting to note that-- how rampant the anti-Semitism in, in Slovakia was.
Not in Czech land-- the Czechs are very-- I must add them to the list of, [LAUGHS] of decent people.

The, the-- Tiso, who was the-- was a priest, who was the head of the Slovakian puppet German regime-- Today, we have got Slovakia independent for the second time in its history, the same as Croatia; they were only independent states during Hitler time-- was the only person-- and he was a Catholic priest, if you please-- who paid the German treasury money for each Jew they deported.
Not even the Austrians did that.
Probably they are too stingy for it.
He literally paid the German treasury for every Jew they deported.
What sort of priest is that?
What sort of person is that who pays to kill other people but under any state?
As it happens, he was hanged after the war.
And today, they are rehabilitating him.
People don't change.
Take it from me.
I'm not suffering from any emotional disturbance.
I'm not having any particular animosity more than necessary.
And I say to you that to reeducate people takes a hell of a long time.
And people are very, very hard to change.
I would say, probably, coming strangely from me, the most reeducation post-war I think happened in Germany.
Germans are probably much larger thinking than some of these small European countries.
They are too small-minded to change.
And they are too nationalistic.
Germans are also nationalistic, but maybe in a larger context.
They want to rule the whole of Europe.
They're not interested just to control the minorities.
And really, things haven't changed.
I, I, I am very hard put to believe that either in Hungary or in Slovakia or Austria-- where I go very often and I know the people; I speak the language-- people have changed.
Yes, there was a period from '45 to early '60s, mid-'60s, when they kept quiet, and it was always the other one who did it.
And they don't know about it.
And they are surprised, and they are so sorry.
And when they met you, they couldn't say enough terrible sort of excuses how such terrible thing could have happened, and they just didn't know about it.
Well, I have yet to believe that the whole of continent of Europe didn't know what was going on.
Anyway, so that is as far as the Slovakian jury is concerned.
And then I would like to proceed as to what was happening in our town and to my knowledge-- actual, personal knowledge of it.
In the late '30s-- and particularly there was an emphasis put on by the Hungarian government, the [NON-ENGLISH] governed with various types of prime ministers.
Some were more anti-Semitic than others.
There were better ones.
[NON-ENGLISH] he was better than [NON-ENGLISH].
You know, this, of course, has changed.
That all Hungarian Jews had to prove their Hungarian nationality.
And that was not a mean task, because not everybody-- those days, people didn't travel.
They didn't have passports.
We did, as it happened.
We used to go every year-- summer holidays to summering in Austria.
And that is, just as I touched it, we used to go there every year.
The last time we were there was in 1937.
And it was a place called Villa [NON-ENGLISH].
It is-- the [NON-ENGLISH], which is the main hotel, very close by next to the [NON-ENGLISH].
I can draw you a picture.
In 1965, I drive up there.
I was in Vienna.
And I want to show my wife, who was alone born in Vienna.
And at the age of two, her parents escaped from Austria and went to Belgium and then to Australia.
I say look, this is the place.
We used to have an apartment on this floor-- tall, sort of Austrian-style holiday resort-- flat.
And this is where we used to go.
While I was there, I speak to the person in charge of it.
This time, it was already a pensione, you know, where they let rooms, not flats anymore.

So I remembered that my father, in 1937, as we got into the fiacre-- you know, that is the horse-drawn sort of carriage which we went to the station with-- that we don't know whether we would be able to come back here next year; it was in the summer of '37-- because the owners have got already a swastika flag.
So in '65 when I am there, I go in.
I speak the language, no problem.
I said, in good German, [SPEAKING GERMAN], Who are the owners?
Oh, she says-- because I said, as a child, I used to come here on holiday.
I didn't say when or what.
So she didn't know.
So she says oh, yes, they are the same people.
They live in Graz.
Graz is [LAUGHS] in the southern part of Austria.
[LAUGHS] It is like all other parts of-- [LAUGHS] most other parts of Austria, it's a hotbed of Nazism.
And just to show you, nothing has changed as far as they are concerned.
Yes, the government is trying to say whatever.
And there are people, maybe of the later generation, who are not in that way inclined.
But the problem is, they've got to live with the knowledge that their fathers, their grandfathers, their cousins, their uncles, they're all killers, one way or the other.
You know, as I said to you when we had our pre-interview discussion, that unfortunately, we Jews are accused that we have a chip on our shoulder.
But I think if you really look at it in the contemporary history of my times, that there were really three sort of non-Jews.
There were the ones who had the opportunity to kill and did.
The ones who didn't have the opportunity, but we don't know if they would have killed, and probably yes.
And then there are some of the really [NON-ENGLISH], you call the the, the, the Righteous Gentiles in small minority.
And I'm not saying that they all did it for money.
Some maybe did.
Nevertheless, even if they did it for money, they were a lifesaver.

Unfortunately, in Hungary, the numbers of individuals who were hidden in percentage was minute compared even to Poland-- which Poland is certainly not known to be [LAUGHS] a nation of friends of Jews.
And therefore, what I want to make on this point is that we lived in an-- in an alien circumstances, believing that we are part of the country.
I must say that, in my time, we-- whilst we were decent citizens and everything else, we did not anymore believe that they are really taking care of us, or they would defend us in any way.
However, we believed that it won't happen to us because of the gradual, step-by-step turning the screw.

And this is-- was the basic method of Germany, of, of, of the Nazis, if you like to call, whereby they did not alert the world, except of maybe some very keen journalists of which stories are who worked in Berlin in, in the '30s, or in any of these German-occupied countries, who were saying what was going truthfully on.

And you know, the bottom line is-- and I've been through Auschwitz-- which is Birkenau, Auschwitz, too, where most of the killing was done-- in Buchenwald, in Reinsdorf, [NON-ENGLISH], and then back to Buchenwald.
And I've seen probably more dead people than I care to remember.
However, the organization of it was absolutely unbelievable.

How they were able to hide the facts, how outwardly it was done in a more or less gentlemanly measure, at least in, in Hungary between the 19th of March 1944, and the 4th of July, when our transport was one before the last.
There was only one more transport from [NON-ENGLISH], which was the internment camp, which came.

Literally, within three months, or three-- exactly three-and-a-half months' time to move and organize over 650,000 people in the midst of the war which they were losing-- you cannot do that unless the local gendarme, who were terrible, police, political parties, and population actively supports it.
If there would have been the least amount of, of, of sympathy, if you like, that we want-- we don't like that to happen to our fellow citizens, they would not have had the time to do it anymore.
And that is where I mentioned the, the, the responsibility of the Judenrat of Budapest, which was, in turn, carried out by the local Judenrat, where they denied the knowledge of the death camps.
They, they assured everybody all is well.
They full well knew the facts.
They were negotiating for their own safety, which, in the end, 1,200 people did get out of Budapest-- all the leaders, and so on.
But there is a great responsibility lying on those people, who may be dead or may be still alive, who have sold out the provincial jury of entire Hungary.
Because I would like to compare them to the man who was the head of the Judenrat in Poland in Warsaw.
When he got to know-- and the Germans were doing it also, giving the Jews instructions.
You do that.
If you don't, you are-- we shoot you.
And so therefore, to make them for their own safety carry out the instructions.

When he got to know that Treblinka-- which-- where the Warsaw Jews were in the main deported to-- existed, he let it be known to the-- to the Jews in the ghetto, or generally to the Polish Jews, he, he made it.
He knew that was a death sentence for him because he, he-- that was the worst thing he could have done, as far as the Germans were concerned, to create panic, and committed suicide.
This man I respect not because I think he should have committed suicide.
But he probably was not in a position to escape.
And so he, he preferred it, before he gets tortured and killed, to do it.
This is not for me to comment.
But that is a man of integrity.
And unfortunately, the Hungarian Jewish leadership did not have this integrity.
They can have thousands of excuses to save themselves, to save their families, fine.
I accept it.
Maybe I wouldn't have been different.
But in that case, resign, and don't carry on the public duty.
You have responsibility to 650,000 people.
Because if they would have said, go and find a rat hole to disappear to, people-- more people would have had the opportunity partially or maybe totally to survive.
Look, there were people who lived on the southern part of Hungary, which was near to Yugoslavia, where, where already in 1944, a lot of partisan action was going on.
It would have been-- young men of military age-- would've easily infiltrated.
So they may have shot 75 of 100.
Then 25 would have survived.
And this is my point on that.
